abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) with long context windows enable powerful
applications but come at the cost of high memory consumption to store the Key
and Value states (KV-Cache). Recent studies attempted to merge KV-cache from
multiple layers into shared representations, yet these approaches either
require expensive pretraining or rely on assumptions of high per-token cosine
similarity across layers which generally does not hold in practice. We find
that the dominant singular vectors are remarkably well-aligned across multiple
layers of the KV-Cache. Exploiting this insight, we propose xKV, a simple
post-training method that applies Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) on the
KV-Cache of grouped layers. xKV consolidates the KV-Cache of multiple layers
into a shared low-rank subspace, significantly reducing KV-Cache sizes. Through
extensive evaluations on the RULER long-context benchmark with widely-used LLMs
(e.g., Llama-3.1 and Qwen2.5), xKV achieves up to 6.8x higher compression rates
than state-of-the-art inter-layer technique while improving accuracy by 2.7%.
Moreover, xKV is compatible with the emerging Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA)
(e.g., DeepSeek-Coder-V2), yielding a notable 3x compression rates on coding
tasks without performance degradation. These results highlight xKV's strong
capability and versatility in addressing memory bottlenecks for long-context
LLM inference. Our code is publicly available at:
this https URL

keyInsights: Dominant singular vectors remain well-aligned across layers even when per-token similarity is low
Keys are more compressible than values, and compression ratios can be task-dependent
The method requires no retraining or architectural modifications
originalProblem: KV-Cache memory consumption becomes a major bottleneck during LLM inference, especially with longer context windows
Existing cross-layer compression methods require expensive model pretraining or make unrealistic assumptions about layer similarities
results: 6.8x higher compression rates compared to MiniCache baseline on RULER benchmark
3x compression achieved on DeepSeek-Coder-V2 without accuracy loss
Compatible with Multi-Head Latent Attention while preserving performance
Successful generalization across multiple model families including Llama-3 and Qwen2.5
solution: Apply SVD across concatenated KV-Caches from multiple layers to identify shared singular vectors
Use stride-based grouping of transformer blocks to share principal components efficiently
Reconstruct compressed KV-Cache using shared singular vector basis with layer-specific matrices
paper_summary.summary: A compression framework enables efficient KV-Cache memory reduction in large language models through cross-layer SVD, achieving up to 6.8x higher compression rates than previous methods while improving accuracy by 2.7% on Llama-3.1-8B and maintaining performance when combined with Multi-Head Latent Attention architectures.

title: xKV: Cross-Layer SVD for KV-Cache Compression

abstract: Scaling language models to handle longer input sequences typically necessitates large key-value (KV) caches, resulting in substantial memory overhead during inference. In this paper, we propose Tensor Product Attention (TPA), a novel attention mechanism that uses tensor decompositions to represent queries, keys, and values compactly, substantially shrinking the KV cache size at inference time. By factorizing these representations into contextual low-rank components and seamlessly integrating with Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), TPA achieves improved model quality alongside memory efficiency. Based on TPA, we introduce the Tensor ProducT ATTenTion Transformer (T6), a new model architecture for sequence modeling. Through extensive empirical evaluation on language modeling tasks, we demonstrate that T6 surpasses or matches the performance of standard Transformer baselines including Multi-Head Attention (MHA), Multi-Query Attention (MQA), Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), and Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) across various metrics, including perplexity and a range of established evaluation benchmarks. Notably, TPA's memory efficiency and computational efficiency at decoding stage enables processing longer sequences under fixed resource constraints, addressing a critical scalability challenge in modern language models. Project Page: this https URL.
title: Tensor Product Attention Is All You Need

abstract: The memory and computational demands of Key-Value (KV) cache present significant challenges for deploying long-context language models. Previous approaches attempt to mitigate this issue by selectively dropping tokens, which irreversibly erases critical information that might be needed for future queries. In this paper, we propose a novel compression technique for KV cache that preserves all token information. Our investigation reveals that: i) Most attention heads primarily focus on the local context; ii) Only a few heads, denoted as retrieval heads, can essentially pay attention to all input tokens. These key observations motivate us to use separate caching strategy for attention heads. Therefore, we propose RazorAttention, a training-free KV cache compression algorithm, which maintains a full cache for these crucial retrieval heads and discards the remote tokens in non-retrieval heads. Furthermore, we introduce a novel mechanism involving a "compensation token" to further recover the information in the dropped tokens. Extensive evaluations across a diverse set of large language models (LLMs) demonstrate that RazorAttention achieves a reduction in KV cache size by over 70% without noticeable impacts on performance. Additionally, RazorAttention is compatible with FlashAttention, rendering it an efficient and plug-and-play solution that enhances LLM inference efficiency without overhead or retraining of the original model.

keyInsights: Not all attention heads in LLMs function identically; some are critical "retrieval heads" for long-range dependencies, while others focus on local context.
Applying different KV cache strategies to specialized heads can achieve substantial memory savings without significant performance loss.
Aggregating information from discarded tokens into a "compensation token" can effectively preserve crucial context for non-retrieval heads.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) face significant memory challenges due to the Key-Value (KV) cache, which grows linearly with context length.
This large KV cache impedes deployment on memory-constrained devices and limits high-throughput serving for long-context LLMs.
Previous KV cache optimization methods, such as token pruning, often discard crucial information, potentially degrading model performance.
results: Achieved over 70% KV cache memory reduction, for instance, 71.8% for Qwen-7B with a 32k token context.
Maintained model performance with less than 1% degradation on most long-context benchmarks like LongBench and Needle In A Haystack.
Outperformed other KV cache compression methods, such as H2O and StreamingLLM, on long-context retrieval tasks while remaining compatible with FlashAttention.
solution: Identifies two types of attention heads: "retrieval heads" (full cache) and "non-retrieval heads" (compressed cache) based on echo and induction scores.
For non-retrieval heads, it maintains a rolling window of recent tokens and creates a "compensation token" to aggregate information from discarded tokens.
The method is training-free, requires only a single forward pass for head identification, and is compatible with existing optimization techniques like FlashAttention.
paper_summary.summary: RazorAttention introduces a selective compression technique for the Key-Value (KV) cache in Large Language Models by distinguishing between retrieval and non-retrieval attention heads. This approach reduces KV cache memory usage by over 70% with less than 1% performance degradation, making LLM deployment more efficient for long contexts.

title: RazorAttention: Efficient KV Cache Compression Through Retrieval Heads

abstract: We demonstrate that geometrically distinctive keys during LLM inference tend to have high attention scores. Based on the phenomenon we propose KeyDiff, a training-free KV cache eviction method based solely on key similarity. Unlike other KV cache eviction methods, KeyDiff can process arbitrarily long prompts within strict resource constraints and efficiently generate responses. We provide a theoretical basis for KeyDiff by relating key diversity with attention scores. These results imply KeyDiff can efficiently identify the most important tokens to retain. Notably KeyDiff does not rely on attention scores, allowing the use of optimized attention mechanisms like FlashAttention. Under a strict memory allowance, we demonstrate the effectiveness of KeyDiff for the Llama and Qwen model families by observing a performance gap of less than 0.04% with 8K cache budget ($\sim$23% KV cache reduction) from the non-evicting baseline on LongBench for Llama 3.1-8B and Llama 3.2-3B. We also observe near baseline performance for Deepseek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on the Math500 reasoning benchmark and decrease end-to-end inference latency by up to 30% compared to the other token-eviction methods.
title: KeyDiff: Key Similarity-Based KV Cache Eviction for Long-Context LLM Inference in Resource-Constrained Environments

abstract: The growing context lengths of large language models (LLMs) pose significant challenges for efficient inference, primarily due to GPU memory and bandwidth constraints. We present RetroInfer, a novel system that reconceptualizes the key-value (KV) cache as a vector storage system which exploits the inherent attention sparsity to accelerate long-context LLM inference. At its core is the wave index, an Attention-aWare VEctor index that enables efficient and accurate retrieval of critical tokens through techniques such as tripartite attention approximation, accuracy-bounded attention estimation, and segmented clustering. Complementing this is the wave buffer, which coordinates KV cache placement and overlaps computation and data transfer across GPU and CPU to sustain high throughput. Unlike prior sparsity-based methods that struggle with token selection and hardware coordination, RetroInfer delivers robust performance without compromising model accuracy. Experiments on long-context benchmarks show up to 4.5X speedup over full attention within GPU memory limits and up to 10.5X over sparse attention baselines when KV cache is extended to CPU memory, all while preserving full-attention-level accuracy.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: The inherent sparsity of attention can be effectively exploited without sacrificing accuracy by dynamically identifying important KV vectors via an attention-aware vector index.

Accuracy and system efficiency can be decoupled through a tripartite attention mechanism, which precisely calculates critical attention scores while estimating less important ones with minimal overhead.
Efficient heterogeneous memory management requires asynchronous operations and leveraging temporal locality for GPU caching and spatial locality for lightweight index updates.
originalProblem: GPU memory and bandwidth limitations caused by the linear growth of the KV cache for long-context LLMs, leading to Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors and throughput bottlenecks.
Existing KV cache offloading methods to CPU memory often introduce significant performance degradation due to high PCIe transfer costs and GPU stalls.
Sparse attention techniques struggle to dynamically identify truly important tokens without compromising inference accuracy or efficiently coordinating heterogeneous memory.
results: Matched or closely approximated full attention accuracy across various long-context benchmarks (RULER, LongBench, NIAH up to 1M tokens), outperforming other sparse attention baselines by 3.33% to 21.63%.

Delivered significant throughput improvements, achieving up to 4.5x speedup over full attention when context fits in GPU memory and up to 10.5x speedup over sparse baselines for 1M token contexts offloaded to CPU memory.
Maintained low prefilling latency, only 2%-7% higher than full attention, while efficiently scaling to previously unmanageable context lengths and batch sizes without OOM errors.
solution: Re-architects the KV cache as a vector storage system, introducing the Wave Index for attention-aware vector retrieval and the Wave Buffer for heterogeneous memory management.
Employs a Tripartite Attention Approximation that divides computation into steady (fixed), retrieval (indexed), and estimation (centroid-approximated) zones to balance accuracy and efficiency.
Manages heterogeneous memory (GPU/CPU) with a GPU-side KV block cache and an asynchronous CPU-side buffer manager, optimizing data movement and computation overlap.
paper_summary.summary: A new system called RetroInfer from Microsoft Research and collaborating universities re-architects the KV cache for large language models into a vector storage system, enabling scalable and accurate inference over extremely long contexts. It delivers full-attention-level accuracy and up to 10.5x faster throughput compared to sparse attention baselines for contexts up to a million tokens.

title: RetroInfer: A Vector-Storage Approach for Scalable Long-Context LLM Inference

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer inference-time memory bottlenecks dominated by the attention Key-Value (KV) cache, which scales with model size and context length. While KV-cache quantization alleviates this cost, bit allocation between keys and values is often tuned heuristically, lacking theoretical grounding and generalizability. This paper proposes two theorems that anchor mixed-precision KV quantization in the intrinsic geometry of Transformer models. First, key projections systematically have larger spectral and Frobenius norms than value matrices, implying higher information density along the key path. Second, for any given memory budget, prioritizing precision for keys over values strictly reduces quantization error and better preserves accuracy. Empirical evaluations across various prominent LLMs and benchmarks show that key-favored allocations (e.g., 4-bit keys, 2-bit values) retain up to 98.3\% accuracy compared to uniform allocations (e.g., 4-bit for both), while conserving memory. These results transform bit allocation from ad hoc tuning into a theoretically grounded, geometry-driven design principle for efficient LLM inference. Source code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Key projection matrices and their resulting activations consistently exhibit larger spectral and Frobenius norms and higher information density compared to value components, rendering them more sensitive to quantization errors.
For any given memory budget, prioritizing higher precision for keys over values rigorously minimizes overall quantization error, leading to better preservation of model accuracy.

The proposed geometry-driven 'More for Keys, Less for Values' principle is orthogonal and synergistic with existing KV cache optimization techniques like outlier redistribution, enabling cumulative performance gains.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) face an inference-time memory bottleneck due to the rapidly growing attention Key-Value (KV) cache, especially with longer context lengths.
Existing mixed-precision KV cache quantization methods lack a strong theoretical foundation, often relying on ad hoc heuristics or empirical tuning for bit allocation between keys and values.
Fixed-precision quantization methods treat keys and values identically, neglecting their potentially distinct roles and sensitivities to compression.
results: Key-favored configurations (e.g., K4V2) consistently outperformed value-favored (K2V4) or uniform (K4V4) bit allocations, recovering approximately 94% of full-precision accuracy on GSM8K with Optimum Quanto and achieving up to +48 percentage points gain for Qwen3-8B on GSM8K at ultra-low precisions with HQQ.
The K4V2 configuration retained 98.3% of the K4V4 baseline's accuracy while achieving a 25% memory reduction for the KV cache compared to uniform 4-bit quantization.

Integration with QuaRot demonstrated that applying rotation specifically to keys yielded greater accuracy improvements than to values, confirming the disproportionate impact of key outliers on quantization distortion.
solution: The authors developed two novel theorems: the 'Key-Value Norm Disparity Theorem' and the 'Key-Prioritized Quantization Theorem,' providing a theoretical basis for differential bit allocation in KV caches.

They propose a geometry-driven design principle, 'More for Keys, Less for Values,' which prioritizes higher precision for key caches due to their intrinsically larger norms and greater information density.
The method was thoroughly validated through extensive empirical evaluations across diverse LLM architectures, multiple downstream tasks, and two hardware-aligned quantization backends.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Case Western Reserve University, Rice University, and Meta developed a geometry-driven principle for mixed-precision KV cache quantization, demonstrating that allocating higher precision to keys than values rigorously reduces quantization error and preserves model accuracy. This approach enables substantial memory savings, improving LLM inference efficiency across diverse models and tasks.

title: Quantize What Counts: More for Keys, Less for Values

abstract: The linear growth of key-value (KV) cache memory and quadratic computational in attention mechanisms complexity pose significant bottlenecks for large language models (LLMs) in long-context processing. While existing KV cache optimization methods address these challenges through token pruning or feature merging, they often incur irreversible information loss or require costly parameter retraining. To this end, we propose ZSMerge, a dynamic KV cache compression framework designed for efficient cache management, featuring three key operations: (1) fine-grained memory allocation guided by multi-dimensional token importance metrics at head-level granularity, (2) a residual merging mechanism that preserves critical context through compensated attention scoring, and (3) a zero-shot adaptation mechanism compatible with diverse LLM architectures without requiring retraining. ZSMerge significantly enhances memory efficiency and inference speed with negligible performance degradation across LLMs. When applied to LLaMA2-7B, it demonstrates a 20:1 compression ratio for key-value cache retention (reducing memory footprint to 5\% of baseline) while sustaining comparable generation quality, coupled with triple throughput gains at extreme 54k-token contexts that eliminate out-of-memory failures. The code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: A tripartite KV cache budget allocation effectively balances local context, global saliency, and compressed historical information to manage memory efficiently.
Merging evicted tokens into a 'residual' component provides a superior method for preserving historical context over simple eviction strategies, significantly reducing attention approximation error.
Compensating attention scores by incorporating token fusion counts stabilizes the attention output, mitigating quality degradation from compressed representations without requiring model retraining.
originalProblem: Linear growth of KV cache memory in LLMs leads to out-of-memory (OOM) errors and limits processing of very long contexts.
Existing KV cache management solutions often involve irreversible information loss from token eviction or introduce significant overhead through retraining and architectural modifications.
A need for a practical, zero-shot KV cache compression method that is compatible with diverse LLM architectures and maintains high generation quality.
results: Achieved up to 20:1 KV cache compression (5% memory footprint) for LLMs like LLaMA2-7B, effectively eliminating OOM errors and enabling processing of extreme contexts up to 54K tokens.

Demonstrated up to a 3x increase in inference throughput for 54K-token contexts, sustaining 9 tokens/second decoding on LLaMA2-7B, and uniquely processed memory-intensive scenarios (e.g., LLaMA2-13B, batch size 16) where other methods failed.

Maintained or slightly improved generation quality on XSum summarization and consistently matched or closely tracked FullKV baselines on the LongBench and InfiniteBench benchmarks across multiple LLM architectures (e.g., LLaMA2, Mistral, Qwen2.5, Yi), retaining up to 96.8% of performance.
solution: Introduces an adaptive budget allocation for the KV cache, segmenting it into proximity (latest tokens), context (salient tokens), and residual (compressed historical tokens) components.
Employs a residual token merging mechanism where evicted tokens are dynamically aggregated into dedicated residual slots via incremental mean aggregation, preventing irreversible information loss.
Applies a compensated attention scoring mechanism that adjusts attention scores based on token fusion counts to correct representation bias and maintain attention distribution coherence.
paper_summary.summary: ZSMerge is a zero-shot KV cache compression framework enabling memory-efficient long-context processing in Large Language Models without requiring fine-tuning or architectural changes. It achieves up to 20:1 KV cache compression and triples inference throughput on LLaMA2-7B for extreme contexts while maintaining or slightly improving generation quality across various benchmarks.

title: ZSMerge: Zero-Shot KV Cache Compression for Memory-Efficient Long-Context LLMs

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown remarkable comprehension abilities but face challenges in GPU memory usage during inference, hindering their scalability for real-time applications like chatbots. To accelerate inference, we store computed keys and values (KV cache) in the GPU memory. Existing methods study the KV cache compression to reduce memory by pruning the pre-computed KV cache. However, they neglect the inter-layer dependency between layers and huge memory consumption in pre-computation. To explore these deficiencies, we find that the number of crucial keys and values that influence future generations decreases layer by layer and we can extract them by the consistency in attention weights. Based on the findings, we propose PyramidInfer, a method that compresses the KV cache by layer-wise retaining crucial context. PyramidInfer saves significant memory by computing fewer keys and values without sacrificing performance. Experimental results show PyramidInfer improves 2.2x throughput compared to Accelerate with over 54% GPU memory reduction in KV cache.

keyInsights: Deep transformer layers exhibit higher redundancy in their KV cache states, while shallow layers retain more critical context information, supporting a layer-wise compression strategy.
An ensemble of attention weights from recent tokens provides a consistent and reliable "oracle" for identifying pivotal context (PvC) crucial for subsequent token generation.
Proactively compressing the KV cache during the prefill phase is essential to mitigate Out-Of-Memory issues and enable the inference of larger models and longer sequences.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from high GPU memory consumption due to the Key-Value (KV) cache during inference, limiting throughput and scalability.
Existing KV cache compression techniques primarily operate after the initial prompt (prefill phase), causing Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors before compression can occur for long prompts or large models.
Many compression approaches apply uniform strategies across all transformer layers, failing to account for varying information importance and redundancy across shallow and deep layers.
results: Achieved up to 2.24x higher inference throughput and reduced KV cache GPU memory by 54.6% for LLaMA 2-13B compared to baselines.
Enabled successful inference of LLaMA 2-70B on an A100 80GB GPU, a scenario where leading methods like Accelerate, Deepspeed, and H2O encountered Out-Of-Memory errors.

Maintained or slightly improved generation quality across a range of LLMs and tasks (e.g., LLaMA 2, Vicuna, CodeLLaMA on MMLU, BBH, GSM8K, MT-Bench), demonstrating superior context retention compared to local-only strategies.
solution: Introduces PyramidInfer, a layer-wise KV cache compression strategy based on observations of Inference Context Redundancy (ICR) and Recent Attention Consistency (RAC).
Implements dynamic selection of "Pivotal Contexts" (PvCs) within the KV cache, using an ensemble of recent token attention weights as an oracle.

Applies a "pyramid" structure for KV cache retention, where shallow layers preserve more context and deeper layers are more aggressively compressed, effectively operating in both prefill and generation phases.
paper_summary.summary: PyramidInfer introduces a pyramid-shaped, layer-wise KV cache compression technique that operates during both the prefill and generation phases of LLM inference. This method achieves up to 2.24x higher throughput and over 54% KV cache memory reduction, notably enabling the inference of LLaMA 2-70B on a single A100 80GB GPU while maintaining generation quality.

title: PyramidInfer: Pyramid KV Cache Compression for High-throughput LLM Inference

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities but face substantial serving costs due to their high memory demands, with the key-value (KV) cache being a primary bottleneck. State-of-the-art KV cache compression techniques, such as quantization and pruning, apply uniform treatment to both keys and values, and discard unimportant tokens entirely, overlooking the fine-grained distinctions in the significance of individual KV cache components. To address such limitations, we introduce \textit{DiffKV}, a novel framework for efficient KV cache compression that exploits three levels of differentiation in the KV cache: (1) the differing impact of keys and values on attention computation, (2) the varying importance of tokens, and (3) the diverse dynamic sparsity patterns across attention heads. These levels of differentiation introduce irregular memory usage patterns across different requests and attention heads, posing significant scalability challenges for memory management. To address these challenges, DiffKV proposes an on-GPU memory manager that compacts fragmented free memory list into contiguous regions in parallel, effectively translating sparsity in the KV cache into performance gains. We evaluate DiffKV on several mainstream LLMs, including the emerging thinking models that generate extended chains of thought. DiffKV is able to compress the KV cache by $2.7\times$ to $5.7\times$ with near-lossless accuracy on complex workloads requiring sophisticated reasoning and long-generation capabilities, and enhances throughput by $1.9\times$ to $5.4\times$. Source codes of DiffKV are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Key vectors, which determine attention scores, have a more critical impact on attention computation than value vectors, justifying differentiated precision assignments.
Token importance and attention sparsity patterns are highly dynamic and vary across different attention heads and requests, necessitating an adaptive, fine-grained compression strategy.
To translate theoretical memory savings into practical performance gains, it is essential to manage irregular memory usage directly on the GPU with highly parallel and low-overhead mechanisms.
originalProblem: KV cache memory consumes over 90% of total memory during LLM inference, scaling linearly with sequence length and concurrent requests, which limits batch sizes and increases latency.

Existing KV cache optimization techniques apply uniform quantization or pruning, overlooking the dynamic and heterogeneous importance of different KV cache components (keys vs. values, individual tokens, attention heads).
Managing the highly irregular memory usage patterns that result from fine-grained, adaptive compression on the GPU poses significant scalability challenges, potentially negating efficiency gains.
results:
Achieved KV cache compression ratios ranging from 2.7 
 to 5.7 
 with an average accuracy degradation of only 0.3% compared to FP16 baselines across diverse benchmarks and models.

Boosted end-to-end throughput by 1.9 
 to 5.4 
 compared to vLLM, supporting substantially larger batch sizes and reducing memory management latency by up to three orders of magnitude.
Demonstrated robust performance for long-context and complex Chain-of-Thought generation tasks, maintaining FP16-comparable quality where other methods experienced severe accuracy drops.
solution: A differentiated KV cache compression policy that assigns higher precision to keys than values, and hierarchically compresses tokens into high-precision, low-precision, or pruned categories based on dynamic importance and per-head sparsity.
An on-GPU memory manager employing parallel KV compaction, utilizing unified pages, a circular free page list, and a bidirectional page table for efficient, dynamic memory allocation and recycling.

A custom GPU attention kernel specifically designed to efficiently compute attention over the differentiated KV cache, leveraging reduced memory access volume and tailored data layouts for acceleration.
paper_summary.summary: DiffKV introduces a framework for large language models that optimizes Key-Value (KV) cache memory through differentiated compression and an on-GPU parallel compaction manager. This approach achieves substantial memory compression and throughput improvements with minimal accuracy degradation, particularly for complex reasoning and long-context generation tasks.

title: DiffKV: Differentiated Memory Management for Large Language Models with Parallel KV Compaction

abstract: Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs) have become increasingly important. However, due to the quadratic time complexity of attention computation, scaling LLMs to longer contexts incurs extremely slow inference speed and high GPU memory consumption for caching key-value (KV) vectors. This paper proposes RetrievalAttention, a training-free approach to both accelerate attention computation and reduce GPU memory consumption. By leveraging the dynamic sparsity of attention mechanism, RetrievalAttention proposes to build approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) indexes for KV vectors in CPU memory and retrieve the most relevant ones through vector search during generation. Unfortunately, we observe that the off-the-shelf ANNS indexes are often ineffective for such retrieval tasks due to the out-of-distribution (OOD) between query vectors and key vectors in the attention mechanism. RetrievalAttention addresses the OOD challenge by designing an attention-aware vector search algorithm that can adapt to the distribution of query vectors. Our evaluation demonstrates that RetrievalAttention achieves near full attention accuracy while only requiring access to 1--3% of the data. This leads to a significant reduction in the inference cost of long-context LLMs, with a much lower GPU memory footprint. In particular, RetrievalAttention only needs a single NVIDIA RTX4090 (24GB) to serve 128K tokens for LLMs with 8B parameters, which is capable of generating one token in 0.188 seconds.

keyInsights: LLM attention is dynamically sparse, with only a small, changing fraction of key-value pairs being critical for generating the current output token.
Off-the-shelf Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) indexes are inefficient for LLM attention due to a fundamental 'out-of-distribution' problem between query and key vectors.
By explicitly accounting for the query-to-key relationship during index construction, vector retrieval can be made highly efficient and accurate for attention computation.
originalProblem: The quadratic time and memory complexity of the attention mechanism and Key-Value (KV) cache causes slow inference and prohibitive GPU memory consumption for long-context LLMs.
Existing static or heuristic sparse attention methods lead to significant accuracy degradation due to the dynamic nature of critical tokens.
Prior KV cache offloading or compression techniques incur high latency due to slow data transfers or struggle with accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.
results: RetrievalAttention achieves near full attention accuracy (e.g., 48.9-49.6% on ∞-Bench for Llama-3-8B) while outperforming static/heuristic methods in accuracy.
It reduces per-token generation latency by up to 4.9x compared to exact KNN (Flat) on an RTX4090 for 128K context, achieving 0.188 seconds per token for Llama-3-8B.
The method enables 8B-parameter LLMs with 128K context to run on a single commodity GPU (NVIDIA RTX4090 with 24GB memory) by efficiently offloading the majority of KV cache to CPU.
solution: The method approximates full attention by selectively retrieving and utilizing only the most relevant KV vectors, dividing the KV cache into a small GPU-resident 'predictable' subset and a larger CPU-offloaded 'dynamic' subset.

It introduces an attention-aware vector search algorithm that builds explicit query-to-key (Q-to-K) KNN connections during index construction, effectively projecting these connections onto key vectors to address the query-key distribution gap.

A CPU-GPU co-execution strategy is employed, where the GPU handles attention on the predictable KV cache in parallel with the CPU performing vector searches and partial attention computations on the dynamic KV vectors, with results combined for final output.
paper_summary.summary: RetrievalAttention introduces an attention-aware vector retrieval method that significantly accelerates long-context Large Language Model inference by offloading most Key-Value cache to CPU memory. This approach enables 8B-parameter LLMs with 128K context to run on a single commodity GPU while maintaining near full attention accuracy and reducing per-token generation latency by up to 4.9x.

title: RetrievalAttention: Accelerating Long-Context LLM Inference via Vector Retrieval

abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have shown promising accuracy improvements on complex problem-solving tasks. While these models have attained high accuracy by leveraging additional computation at test time, they need to generate long chain-of-thought reasoning in order to think before answering, which requires generating thousands of tokens. While sparse attention methods can help reduce the KV cache pressure induced by this long autoregressive reasoning, these methods can introduce errors which disrupt the reasoning process. Additionally, prior methods often pre-process the input to make it easier to identify the important prompt tokens when computing attention during generation, and this pre-processing is challenging to perform online for newly generated reasoning tokens. Our work addresses these challenges by introducing Multipole Attention, which accelerates autoregressive reasoning by only computing exact attention for the most important tokens, while maintaining approximate representations for the remaining tokens. Our method first performs clustering to group together semantically similar key vectors, and then uses the cluster centroids both to identify important key vectors and to approximate the remaining key vectors in order to retain high accuracy. We design a fast cluster update process to quickly re-cluster the input and previously generated tokens, thereby allowing for accelerating attention to the previous output tokens. We evaluate our method using emerging LRMs such as Qwen-8B, demonstrating that our approach can maintain accuracy on complex reasoning tasks even with aggressive attention sparsity settings. We also provide kernel implementations to demonstrate the practical efficiency gains from our method, achieving up to 4.5$\times$ speedup for attention in long-context reasoning applications. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: Multipole Attention for Efficient Long Context Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in long-context tasks such as reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn dialogue. However, inference over extended contexts is bottlenecked by the Key-Value (KV) cache, whose memory footprint grows linearly with sequence length and dominates latency at each decoding step. While recent KV cache compression methods identify and load important tokens, they focus predominantly on input contexts and fail to address the cumulative attention errors that arise during long decoding. In this paper, we introduce RetroAttention, a novel KV cache update technique that retrospectively revises past attention outputs using newly arrived KV entries from subsequent decoding steps. By maintaining a lightweight output cache, RetroAttention enables past queries to efficiently access more relevant context, while incurring minimal latency overhead. This breaks the fixed-attention-output paradigm and allows continual correction of prior approximations. Extensive experiments on long-generation benchmarks show that RetroAttention consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) KV compression methods, increasing effective KV exposure by up to 1.6$\times$ and accuracy by up to 21.9\%.
title: Retrospective Sparse Attention for Efficient Long-Context Generation

abstract: Key-Value (KV) caching is a common technique to enhance the computational efficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs), but its memory overhead grows rapidly with input length. Prior work has shown that not all tokens are equally important for text generation, proposing layer-level KV cache compression to selectively retain key information. Recognizing the distinct roles of attention heads in generation, we propose HeadKV, a head-level KV cache compression method, and HeadKV-R2, which leverages a novel contextual reasoning ability estimation for compression. Our approach operates at the level of individual heads, estimating their importance for contextual QA tasks that require both retrieval and reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks (LongBench, LooGLE), model architectures (e.g., Llama-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct), and long-context abilities tests demonstrate that our head-level KV cache compression significantly outperforms strong baselines, particularly in low-resource settings (KV size = 64 & 128). Notably, our method retains just 1.5% of the KV cache while achieving 97% of the performance of the full KV cache on the contextual question answering benchmark. Codes are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Leveraging the specialized functions of individual attention heads through fine-grained, head-level KV cache management is more effective than coarser-grained compression for LLM efficiency.
Effective KV cache compression for complex long-context tasks requires importance estimation to account for both information retrieval and multi-step reasoning capabilities of attention heads.

Dynamically allocating KV cache budget at the head level, guided by task-specific importance, enables substantial memory savings while preserving, or sometimes enhancing, model performance on relevant tasks.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) handling long input contexts face high memory usage and latency due to the Key-Value (KV) cache, which grows linearly with context length.

Existing KV cache compression methods primarily use coarse-grained strategies (e.g., token eviction, layer-level allocation), failing to leverage the specialized functions of individual attention heads.

Prior importance estimation methods, like those based on simple "Needle-in-a-Haystack" tests, focus only on retrieval and are insufficient for complex tasks requiring both information retrieval and contextual reasoning.
results: HeadKV-R2 consistently outperformed strong baselines (SnapKV, PyramidKV, Ada-SnapKV) on contextual QA tasks from LongBench and LooGLE, using Llama-3-8B-Instruct and Mistral-7B-Instruct models.
The method achieved 97% of the performance of a full KV cache on contextual question answering benchmarks while retaining only 1.5% of the KV cache (e.g., 128 tokens for an average 8683 token input).

HeadKV-R2 demonstrated superior long-context retrieval (e.g., 98.20 average score on Needle-in-a-Haystack for Llama-3-8B-Instruct with KV size 128) and reasoning abilities, in some cases slightly surpassing full KV cache performance on complex reasoning tasks.
solution: Developed HeadKV-R2, a head-level KV cache compression method that introduces a novel Retrieval-Reasoning (R2) importance estimation by constructing structured examples requiring both retrieval and multi-step reasoning.
Implemented a dynamic KV cache allocation strategy that distributes budget to individual attention heads proportional to their R2 importance scores, moving beyond fixed layer-level budgeting.

Employed an attention-based KV cache selection strategy within each head's dynamically allocated budget, prioritizing key-value pairs with higher aggregated attention scores from local instruction tokens.
paper_summary.summary: HeadKV-R2 introduces a head-level KV cache compression method for large language models, dynamically allocating memory based on each attention head's contribution to retrieval and reasoning tasks. This approach achieves 97% of the full KV cache performance on contextual question answering benchmarks while retaining only 1.5% of the cache, significantly boosting efficiency in low-resource settings.

title: Not All Heads Matter: A Head-Level KV Cache Compression Method with Integrated Retrieval and Reasoning

abstract: Language models struggle to generalize beyond pretraining context lengths, limiting long-horizon reasoning and retrieval. Continued pretraining on long-context data can help but is expensive due to the quadratic scaling of Attention. We observe that most tokens do not require (Global) Attention over the entire sequence and can rely on local context. Based on this, we propose L2A (Learning To Attend), a layer that enables conditional (token-wise) long-range memory access by deciding when to invoke global attention. We evaluate L2A on Qwen 2.5 and Qwen 3 models, extending their effective context length from 32K to 128K tokens. L2A matches the performance of standard long-context training to within 3% while skipping Global Attention for $\sim$80% of tokens, outperforming prior baselines. We also design custom Triton kernels to efficiently implement this token-wise conditional Attention on GPUs, achieving up to $\sim$2x improvements in training throughput and time-to-first-token over FlashAttention. Moreover, L2A enables post-training pruning of highly sparse Global Attention layers, reducing KV cache memory by up to 50% with negligible performance loss.
title: Learning When to Attend: Conditional Memory Access for Long-Context LLMs

abstract: The quadratic computational complexity of the attention mechanism in current Large Language Models (LLMs) renders inference with long contexts prohibitively expensive. To address this challenge, various approaches aim to retain critical portions of the context to optimally approximate Full Attention (FA) through Key-Value (KV) compression or Sparse Attention (SA), enabling the processing of virtually unlimited text lengths in a streaming manner. However, these methods struggle to achieve performance levels comparable to FA, particularly in retrieval tasks. In this paper, our analysis of attention head patterns reveals that LLMs' attention distributions show strong local correlations, naturally reflecting a chunking mechanism for input context. We propose Ltri-LLM framework, which divides KVs into spans, stores them in an offline index, and retrieves the relevant KVs into memory for various queries. Experimental results on popular long text benchmarks show that Ltri-LLM can achieve performance close to FA while maintaining efficient, streaming-based inference.

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) inherently exhibit strong local correlations in their attention distributions, forming 'triangular regions' that correspond to semantic segments or spans within the input.

Existing streaming inference methods, like InfLLM, face a critical retrieval bottleneck, struggling to recall crucial tokens (even those with low attention scores) from long contexts, which impacts performance in tasks like Needle-In-A-Haystack.

Dynamically adapting the number of index vectors per semantic span based on 'voter confidence' (ratio of a span's attention to its neighbors) can enhance retrieval accuracy while maintaining compression.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur quadratic computational complexity for self-attention and prohibitively large Key-Value (KV) cache memory requirements when processing extremely long input contexts.
Prior streaming inference methods for LLMs, such as InfLLM, struggle with information retrieval tasks from long contexts, often overlooking critical tokens necessary for accurate answers.
There is a need for efficient long-context inference solutions that can approximate the performance of full attention without requiring costly model retraining or fine-tuning.
results: Ltri-LLM achieves superior retrieval accuracy, outperforming InfLLM across Needle-In-A-Haystack tests (20K to 230K context), even when the 'needle' is split across blocks.
It secures the highest total average score on the ∞-Bench benchmark, leading in 4 overall tasks and 8 streaming tasks, and outperforms vanilla LLAMA3-8B-Instruct-262K on Retr.KV.

Ltri-LLM dramatically reduces KV cache GPU memory usage to 0.027 GiB for a 100K context, compared to InfLLM's 2.241 GiB and vanilla LLAMA3-8B-Instruct-262K's 23.344 GiB, demonstrating orders of magnitude improvement in efficiency.
solution: Introduces a training-free framework that identifies semantic spans in long contexts using a Triangle Attention (TA) score and Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) to capture inherent attention patterns.
Generates dynamic span index vectors, where the number of vectors retained for each span is dynamically determined by its 'voter confidence' (a ratio of its TA score to neighboring spans).

Implements a streaming inference framework that offloads full Keys to CPU memory while retaining dynamic index vectors in GPU memory, supplemented by a persistent mechanism, dedicated 'retrieval heads', and collaborative voting across layers to boost retrieval accuracy during decoding.
paper_summary.summary: Ltri-LLM is a training-free framework for streaming long-context inference in Large Language Models that improves retrieval accuracy and reduces Key-Value (KV) cache memory usage by leveraging dynamic triangular attention patterns. It achieves superior performance on long-context benchmarks like ∞-Bench and RULER, while reducing KV cache GPU memory for 100K contexts to 0.027 GiB, orders of magnitude less than prior methods.

title: Ltri-LLM: Streaming Long Context Inference for LLMs with Training-Free Dynamic Triangular Attention Pattern

abstract: Memory and computation remain core bottlenecks in long-horizon LLM inference due to the quadratic cost of self-attention and the ever-growing key-value (KV) cache. Existing strategies for memory-bounded inference, such as quantization, offloading, or heuristic KV eviction, either incur high orchestration costs or rely on unreliable attention-based proxies of importance. We propose TRIM-KV, a novel approach that learns each token's intrinsic importance at creation time via a lightweight retention gate. Each gate predicts a scalar retention score that decays over time, reflecting the long-term utility of the token for a specific layer and head. Tokens with low scores are evicted when the memory budget is exceeded, ensuring that the cache always contains the most critical tokens. TRIM-KV is trained efficiently through distillation from a frozen LLM combined with a capacity loss, requiring only gate fine-tuning and adding negligible inference overhead. Across mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME24), procedural generation (LongProc), conversational long-memory benchmarks (LongMemEval), and long-context understanding (LongBenchV2 and SCBench), TRIM-KV consistently outperforms strong eviction and learnable retrieval baselines, especially in low-memory regimes. Remarkably, it even surpasses full-cache models in some settings, showing that selective retention can serve as a form of regularization, suppressing noise from uninformative tokens. Qualitative analyses further reveal that learned retention scores align with human intuition, naturally recovering heuristics such as sink tokens, sliding windows, and gist compression without explicit design. Beyond efficiency, retention scores provide insights into layer- and head-specific roles, suggesting a new path toward LLM interpretability.
title: Cache What Lasts: Token Retention for Memory-Bounded KV Cache in LLMs

abstract:
Key-value (KV) caching has emerged as a crucial optimization technique for
accelerating inference in large language models (LLMs). By allowing the
attention operation to scale linearly rather than quadratically with the total
sequence length, KV caching significantly enhances generation throughput.
However, due to large context lengths in the modern LLMs, the memory footprint
of the KV is a huge bottleneck for model deployment directly impacting the
model's batch size, hindering its ability to deliver high-throughput. Existing
research addresses this challenge using several techniques, such as discarding
low-attention tokens, quantization, and matrix approximation which typically
lead to a negative impact on the model accuracy.
In this paper, We propose KVCrush technology which can be combined with many
KV compression technologies to improve the model accuracy at a much smaller
memory. KVCrush provides an alternate representation scheme for key-value
states, along with a low-overhead token pruning algorithm that accounts for the
token distribution in the KV cache, which in turn allows for a a smaller
footprint while maintaining the accuracy of the model. Based on our results,
KVCrush reduces LongBench KV Cache size by 4x with less than 1% accuracy drop
and achieves state-of-the-art average accuracy with minimal overhead, incurring
less than 0.5% total inference latency. KVCrush not only outperforms the
accuracy of state-of-the-art importance-based token retention schemes but is
also compatible with typical practical LLM deployments using KV cache paging
schemes such as vLLM and mixed precision quantization.
title: KVCrush: Key value cache size-reduction using similarity in  head-behaviour

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit enhanced capabilities by Chain-of-Thought reasoning. However, the extended reasoning sequences introduce significant GPU memory overhead due to increased key-value (KV) cache. Existing KV cache compression methods mitigate memory bottlenecks but struggle in long reasoning tasks. In this paper, we analyze attention patterns in reasoning tasks and reveal a Token Importance Recurrence phenomenon: a large proportion of tokens regain high attention after multiple decoding steps, which is failed to capture by existing works and may lead to unpredictable eviction on such periodically critical tokens. To address this, we propose LazyEviction, an observation window-based lagged eviction framework retaining latent recurring tokens by prioritized eviction based on tokens' recurrence patterns. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LazyEviction reduces KV cache by 50%~70% while maintaining comparable accuracy, outperforming existing KV cache compression baselines. Our implementation code can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: The Token Importance Recurrence (TIR) phenomenon demonstrates that tokens temporarily exhibiting low attention frequently regain high importance across multiple decoding steps in long reasoning sequences.

Traditional greedy, per-step KV cache eviction strategies are suboptimal for long reasoning as they fail to anticipate and preserve these recurring critical tokens, leading to a loss of "knowledge continuity."
A predictive retention strategy, which observes attention patterns over an interval and leverages recurrence metrics, can more effectively manage KV cache and maintain reasoning accuracy.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur high Key-Value (KV) cache memory overhead during long reasoning tasks, limiting their efficiency and length capacity.
Existing greedy KV cache compression methods prematurely evict tokens that may become important later, leading to accuracy degradation in multi-step reasoning.
The bottleneck of KV cache memory often exceeds GPU capacity for very long sequences, necessitating more effective memory management without compromising reasoning quality.
results: LazyEviction consistently outperformed all tested state-of-the-art KV cache compression methods across diverse reasoning benchmarks, including mathematical (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME), scientific (GPQA), and programming (LiveCodeBench) tasks.
The method achieved a 50%-70% reduction in KV cache memory while maintaining accuracy comparable to, and sometimes exceeding, that of an uncompressed FullKV cache.

For very long reasoning sequences (e.g., 16K tokens), LazyEviction demonstrated improved overall inference efficiency (throughput and average latency) compared to FullKV, showcasing its practical benefits.
solution: LazyEviction implements an Observation Window-based Lagged Eviction Mechanism, deferring eviction decisions to periodic intervals instead of making them at every decoding step.
It introduces Recurrence Interval Tracking (MRI) for each token, which records the longest observed period between consecutive activations to quantify temporal importance.

An MRI-Centric Eviction Policy calculates an importance score based on a token's MRI and time since last activation, guiding the retention of critical recurring tokens while evicting less important ones.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology and the Hong Kong Polytechnic University developed LazyEviction, a KV cache management method that observes attention patterns over time. This approach achieved a 50%-70% KV cache memory reduction and consistently outperformed existing methods in long reasoning tasks across various LLMs, often matching or exceeding the accuracy of an uncompressed cache.

title: LazyEviction: Lagged KV Eviction with Attention Pattern Observation for Efficient Long Reasoning

abstract: The scalability of large language models for long-context reasoning is severely constrained by the linear growth of their Transformer key-value cache, which incurs significant memory and computational costs. We posit that as a model generates reasoning tokens, the informational value of past generated tokens diminishes, creating an opportunity for compression. In this work, we propose to periodically compress the generation KV cache with a learned, special-purpose token and evict compressed entries. We train the model to perform this compression via a modified joint distillation and reinforcement learning (RL) framework. Our training method minimizes overhead over the conventional RL process, as it leverages RL outputs for distillation. Empirically, our method achieves a superior memory-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to both the model without cache compression and training-free compression techniques.
title: Breadcrumbs Reasoning: Memory-Efficient Reasoning with Compression Beacons

abstract:
The deployment of large language models (LLMs) is often hindered by the
extensive memory requirements of the Key-Value (KV) cache, especially as
context lengths increase. Existing approaches to reduce the KV cache size
involve either fine-tuning the model to learn a compression strategy or
leveraging attention scores to reduce the sequence length. We analyse the
attention distributions in decoder-only Transformers-based models and observe
that attention allocation patterns stay consistent across most layers.
Surprisingly, we find a clear correlation between the $L_2$ and the attention
scores over cached KV pairs, where a low $L_2$ of a key embedding usually leads
to a high attention score during decoding. This finding indicates that the
influence of a KV pair is potentially determined by the key embedding itself
before being queried. Based on this observation, we compress the KV cache based
on the $L_2$ of key embeddings. Our experimental results show that this simple
strategy can reduce the KV cache size by 50% on language modelling and
needle-in-a-haystack tasks and 90% on passkey retrieval tasks without losing
accuracy. Moreover, without relying on the attention scores, this approach
remains compatible with FlashAttention, enabling broader applicability.
title: A Simple and Effective $L_2$ Norm-Based Strategy for KV Cache  Compression

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs), despite their remarkable performance across a wide range of tasks, necessitate substantial GPU memory and consume significant computational resources. Beyond the memory taken up by model weights, the memory used by the KV cache rises linearly with sequence length, becoming a primary bottleneck for inference. In this paper, we introduce an innovative method for optimizing the KV cache, which considerably minimizes its memory footprint. Upon thorough investigation, we discover that in most Transformer models, (i) there is a striking similarity between adjacent tokens' query vectors, and (ii) the attention calculation of the current query can rely exclusively on the attention information of a small fraction of preceding queries. Based on these observations, we present CORM, a KV cache eviction policy that dynamically retains essential key-value pairs for inference without the need for model fine-tuning. Our validation shows that CORM reduces the inference memory usage of KV cache by up to 70\% with negligible performance degradation across six tasks in LongBench. Furthermore, we demonstrate that CORM is compatible with GQA for further compression rate.
title: CORM: Cache Optimization with Recent Message for Large Language Model Inference

abstract:
Large Language Models struggle with memory demands from the growing Key-Value
(KV) cache as context lengths increase. Existing compression methods homogenize
head dimensions or rely on attention-guided token pruning, often sacrificing
accuracy or introducing computational overhead. We propose FourierAttention, a
training-free framework that exploits the heterogeneous roles of transformer
head dimensions: lower dimensions prioritize local context, while upper ones
capture long-range dependencies. By projecting the long-context-insensitive
dimensions onto orthogonal Fourier bases, FourierAttention approximates their
temporal evolution with fixed-length spectral coefficients. Evaluations on
LLaMA models show that FourierAttention achieves the best long-context accuracy
on LongBench and Needle-In-A-Haystack (NIAH). Besides, a custom Triton kernel,
FlashFourierAttention, is designed to optimize memory via streamlined
read-write operations, enabling efficient deployment without performance
compromise.

keyInsights: Transformer head dimensions exhibit heterogeneous roles: lower dimensions process local context, while upper dimensions are crucial for long-range dependencies.
The HiPPO-FourierT framework provides a mathematically robust and online method for approximating and compressing sequences using Fourier transforms, suitable for streaming KV cache compression.
A fine-grained, asymmetric compression schema (compressing more V dimensions and more lower-layer dimensions) derived from empirical analysis leads to superior performance.
originalProblem: The Key-Value (KV) cache grows linearly with context length, leading to prohibitive memory consumption for Large Language Models (LLMs), limiting their deployment.

Existing KV cache optimization methods (quantization, sparse attention, token eviction) often compromise accuracy, apply uniform compression, or incur significant computational overhead from attention re-calculations.
Current approaches often treat transformer head dimensions as homogeneous, overlooking their distinct contributions to context processing.
results: FourierAttention achieved 93.17% on NIAH for LLaMA3.1-8B and 94.04% for LLaMA3.2-3B at 32k context, consistently outperforming other methods while compressing 76% of the KV cache.
On LongBench, it maintained performance closest to the original models, surpassing StreamingLLM, Palu, and KIVI across various tasks.

Ablation studies confirmed FourierT's superior reconstruction quality and performance (98.25% NIAH average) compared to LegT, and validated the fine-grained compression schema over uniform approaches.
solution: A training-free framework, FourierAttention, identifies and selectively compresses less context-sensitive transformer head dimensions using an empirical analysis of their heterogeneous roles.
It employs a HiPPO-FourierT (Translated Fourier Transform) based online compression method to project the selected KV cache dimensions into a compact, fixed-length spectral representation.

A fine-grained, asymmetric compression schema prioritizes compressing V dimensions and lower transformer layers, further optimized with a custom FlashFourierAttention kernel for efficient integration with FlashAttention.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University, Huawei Noah's Ark Lab, and Shanghai AI Lab introduce FourierAttention, a training-free framework that achieves significant memory savings for Large Language Models by exploiting the heterogeneous roles of transformer head dimensions. The method compresses up to 76% of the Key-Value cache using Fourier-approximated representations, maintaining high performance on long-context benchmarks like LongBench and Needle-In-A-Haystack, often surpassing existing optimization techniques.

title: Beyond Homogeneous Attention: Memory-Efficient LLMs via  Fourier-Approximated KV Cache

abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) rely on key-value (KV) caches for efficient autoregressive decoding; however, cache size grows linearly with context length and model depth, becoming a major bottleneck in long-context inference. Prior KV cache compression methods either enforce rigid heuristics, disrupt tensor layouts with per-attention-head variability, or require specialized compute kernels.
We propose a simple, yet effective, KV cache compression framework based on attention-guided, layer-adaptive composite tokens. Our method aggregates attention scores to estimate token importance, selects head-specific tokens independently, and aligns them into composite tokens that respect the uniform cache structure required by existing inference engines. A global allocation mechanism further adapts retention budgets across layers, assigning more capacity to layers with informative tokens. This approach achieves significant memory reduction while preserving accuracy, consistently outperforming prior structured and semi-structured methods. Crucially, our approach remains fully compatible with standard inference pipelines, offering a practical and scalable solution for efficient long-context LLM deployment.
title: KVCompose: Efficient Structured KV Cache Compression with Composite Tokens

abstract:
As large language models (LLMs) process increasing context windows, the
memory usage of KV cache has become a critical bottleneck during inference. The
mainstream KV compression methods, including KV pruning and KV quantization,
primarily focus on either token or precision dimension separately. However,
these works leaving the trade-off between these two orthogonal dimensions
largely under-explored. In this paper, we comprehensively investigate the
token-precision trade-off in KV cache compression.Experiments demonstrate that
storing more tokens in the KV cache with lower precision,a strategy we term
quantized pruning, can significantly enhance the long-context performance of
LLMs. In-depth analysis of the token-precision trade-off across key aspects
demonstrates that, quantized pruning achieves substantial improvements in
retrieval-related tasks and consistently performs well across varying input
lengths. Furthermore, quantized pruning demonstrates notable stability and
effectiveness across different KV pruning methods, quantization strategies, and
model scales. These findings offer valuable insights into optimizing KV cache
compression through balanced token-precision trade-off strategies. Our code is
available at this https URL
title: More Tokens, Lower Precision: Towards the Optimal Token-Precision  Trade-off in KV Cache Compression

abstract: The Key-Value (KV) cache introduces substantial memory overhead during large language model (LLM) inference. Although existing vector quantization (VQ) methods reduce KV cache usage and provide flexible representational capacity across bit-widths, they suffer severe performance degradation at ultra-low bit-widths due to key cache outliers that hinder effective codebook utilization. To address this challenge, we propose VecInfer, a novel VQ method for aggressive KV cache compression while enabling efficient inference. By applying smooth and Hadamard transformations, VecInfer suppresses outliers in the key cache, enabling the codebook to comprehensively cover the original data distribution and thereby reducing quantization difficulty. To facilitate efficient deployment, we design an optimized CUDA kernel that fuses computation with dequantization to minimize memory access overhead. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that VecInfer consistently outperforms existing quantization baselines across both long-context understanding and mathematical reasoning tasks. With only 2-bit quantization, VecInfer achieves performance comparable to full precision, while delivering up to $\mathbf{2.7\times}$ speedup in large-batch self-attention computation and $\mathbf{8.3\times}$ reduction in single-batch end-to-end latency on Llama-3.1-8B with a 196k sequence length.

keyInsights: Key cache outliers are a primary cause of severe accuracy degradation in existing VQ methods at aggressive compression ratios.
Applying dual equivalent transformations to keys can effectively suppress outliers and uniformize the data distribution without affecting computational invariance of the attention mechanism.
Integrating dequantization directly into the attention computation kernel with hardware-aware optimizations is critical to translating memory savings into tangible inference speedups.
originalProblem: The Key-Value (KV) cache in large language models (LLMs) consumes substantial memory, growing linearly with context length and limiting batch size.
Existing vector quantization (VQ) methods for KV cache compression suffer severe accuracy degradation at ultra-low bit-widths due to prevalent outliers in the key cache.
Dequantization overhead during LLM inference can negate memory reduction benefits, requiring hardware-aligned optimizations for practical speedups.
results: VecInfer achieves only a 2.1% average accuracy drop at 2-bit quantization on LongBench, outperforming other VQ methods like MILLION by 14.5% and maintaining robust performance at 1.5-bit.
It delivers up to an 8.3x reduction in single-batch end-to-end decoding latency for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (196k sequence length) on an H100 GPU at 2-bit configuration.
The method demonstrates up to 2.8x speedup in large-batch self-attention computation on A100/H100 compared to FP16, with the dual transformation adding negligible overhead.
solution: A novel Outlier-Suppressed Vector Quantization (OSVQ) scheme applies dual equivalent transformations (Smooth and Hadamard) to the key cache before quantization, making its distribution more uniform and outlier-free.
Offline, task-independent codebooks for keys and values are trained using K-Means clustering on pre-sampled, outlier-suppressed data.

A hardware-efficient custom CUDA kernel is designed to fuse dequantization with attention computation, utilizing fine-grained tiling and asynchronous pipelining to reduce global memory access overhead.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Xiaomi Inc. developed VecInfer, a vector quantization method that uses dual equivalent transformations to compress LLM KV cache at ultra-low bit-widths while preserving accuracy. It achieves up to an 8.3x reduction in end-to-end decoding latency and maintains near full-precision performance at 2-bit quantization, even at 1.5-bit where other methods fail.

title: VecInfer: Efficient LLM Inference with Low-Bit KV Cache via Outlier-Suppressed Vector Quantization

abstract: With the rapid development of large language models (LLMs), handling long context has become one of the vital abilities in LLMs. Such long-context ability is accompanied by difficulties in deployment, especially due to the increased consumption of KV cache. There is certain work aiming to optimize the memory footprint of KV cache, inspired by the observation that attention heads can be categorized into retrieval heads that are of great significance and streaming heads that are of less significance. Typically, identifying the streaming heads and and waiving the KV cache in the streaming heads would largely reduce the overhead without hurting the performance that much. However, since employing both retrieval and streaming heads in one layer decomposes one large round of attention computation into two small ones, it may unexpectedly bring extra latency on accessing and indexing tensors. Based on this intuition, we impose an important improvement to the identification process of retrieval and streaming heads, in which we design a criterion that enforces exclusively retrieval or streaming heads gathered in one unique layer. In this way, we further eliminate the extra latency and only incur negligible performance degradation. Our method named \textsc{ZigzagAttention} is competitive among considered baselines owing to reduced latency and comparable performance.
title: ZigzagAttention: Efficient Long-Context Inference with Exclusive Retrieval and Streaming Heads

abstract: The long-output context generation of large reasoning models enables extended chain of thought (CoT) but also drives rapid growth of the key-value (KV) cache, quickly overwhelming GPU memory. To address this challenge, we propose ThinKV, a thought-adaptive KV cache compression framework. ThinKV is based on the observation that attention sparsity reveals distinct thought types with varying importance within the CoT. It applies a hybrid quantization-eviction strategy, assigning token precision by thought importance and progressively evicting tokens from less critical thoughts as reasoning trajectories evolve. Furthermore, to implement ThinKV, we design a kernel that extends PagedAttention to enable efficient reuse of evicted tokens' memory slots, eliminating compaction overheads. Extensive experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill, GPT-OSS, and NVIDIA AceReason across mathematics and coding benchmarks show that ThinKV achieves near-lossless accuracy with less than 5% of the original KV cache, while improving performance with up to 5.8x higher inference throughput over state-of-the-art baselines.

keyInsights: Attention sparsity patterns in LRMs can be used to dynamically decompose the Chain of Thought into distinct types (Reasoning, Execution, Transition), each with varying importance.
Tailoring quantization precision and eviction strategies to these identified thought types allows for aggressive KV cache compression with minimal impact on complex reasoning accuracy.
In-place memory reuse for dynamically evicted tokens, leveraging the permutation invariance of attention, effectively eliminates the performance bottlenecks associated with memory compaction.
originalProblem: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) generate extensive Chains of Thought (CoT), causing the Key-Value (KV) cache to grow linearly and quickly exhaust GPU memory.
Existing KV cache compression methods, such as uniform quantization or recency-based eviction, often degrade LRM accuracy significantly due to their token-agnostic nature.
Dynamic eviction techniques introduce memory fragmentation that necessitates costly gather-based compaction operations, severely impacting inference throughput.
results: ThinKV achieves a KV cache memory footprint of less than 5% of the original size (e.g., <3.67%) while maintaining near-lossless accuracy (<4% drop) on benchmarks like AIME and LiveCodeBench.
It demonstrates up to 5.8x higher inference throughput compared to gather-based R-KV baselines and 3.6x over overlapped gather R-KV on an NVIDIA A100 GPU for R1-Llama-8B.
The framework supports up to 3x larger batch sizes by eliminating gather-based compaction overheads through the Continuous Thinking kernel.
solution: ThinKV introduces a 'thought-adaptive' hybrid quantization-eviction framework that dynamically classifies segments of the Chain of Thought into Reasoning, Execution, or Transition types based on attention sparsity.

It implements 'Think Before you Quantize' (TBQ) to apply higher bit-precision (e.g., 4-bit) to more important thought types (Reasoning, Execution) and lower precision (e.g., 2-bit) to less critical ones (Transition).

The 'Think Before You Evict' (TBE) scheme proactively anneals previous thought segments upon generating a 'Transition' thought, summarizing evicted segments via K-means clustering, and a 'Continuous Thinking' (CT) kernel enables in-place memory reuse to avoid compaction overheads.
paper_summary.summary: ThinKV, developed by researchers at Georgia Tech and NVIDIA, proposes a thought-adaptive hybrid quantization-eviction framework that reduces the KV cache memory footprint of Large Reasoning Models by over 95%. This approach enables up to 5.8x higher inference throughput while maintaining near-lossless reasoning accuracy during long-output context generation.

title: ThinKV: Thought-Adaptive KV Cache Compression for Efficient Reasoning Models

abstract: Quantizing the key-value (KV) cache is a promising strategy for improving the inference efficiency of large language models (LLMs). However, aggressive quantization to very low precision (e.g., 2 bits) introduces significant errors in the stored key and value tensors, which propagate through the dot-product attention mechanism and ultimately degrade generation quality. To address this, we propose KVLinC, a framework to mitigate attention errors introduced by KV cache quantization in the extreme low-precision regime. KVLinC combines a Hadamard rotation, which reduces quantization error in values, with lightweight linear correction adapters that explicitly compensate for errors introduced by quantized keys. Across extensive evaluations on the LLaMA, Qwen2.5, and Qwen3 model families, KVLinC consistently matches or surpasses strong baselines while achieving higher KV-cache compression. Furthermore, we implement a custom attention kernel that results in upto 2.55x faster inference compared to Flash Attention baseline, enabling efficient long-context LLM inference.

keyInsights: Keys and values exhibit distinct statistical properties (e.g., key outliers), necessitating different quantization strategies to maximize precision.
Applying Hadamard rotation before key/value formation (pre-multiplication) is detrimental, while post-multiplication (rotating channels within each token) is more stable for quantization.

Linear correction adapters, trained to align quantized attention weights with full-precision ones, can effectively mitigate accumulated errors from low-precision keys with minimal parameter and memory overhead.
originalProblem: The KV cache in LLMs creates a significant memory and bandwidth bottleneck during inference, especially for long sequence lengths.
Aggressively low-precision quantization (e.g., 2-bit) of the KV cache introduces substantial errors, degrading generation quality and limiting practical memory savings.
Existing quantization methods either struggle with accuracy at extreme low precision or introduce additional memory overheads that diminish overall compression benefits for long contexts.
results: KVLinC achieved up to 6.4% higher GSM8K accuracy and 2.3% higher BBH accuracy compared to Gear-L, with significant gains for smaller models like Llama-2-7B.
The method enabled up to 3.5x larger batch sizes and 2.55x inference throughput for Llama models compared to FP16 FlashAttention-2, reducing KV cache memory footprint by 8x.
It demonstrated improved performance on long-context benchmarks like RULER and IF-Eval for instruction-tuned models, achieving over 10% improvement on RULER for Qwen-2.5-3B-Instruct.
solution: KVLinC employs a tailored 2-bit quantization strategy that quantizes raw keys channel-wise and Hadamard-rotated values token-wise, based on an extensive ablation study.
Lightweight, trainable linear correction adapters are introduced for keys to compensate for residual quantization errors, augmenting the attention mechanism with recurrently updated additive terms.
A custom Triton-based attention decoding kernel is implemented, optimized for direct execution on quantized operands, to achieve end-to-end hardware acceleration and memory efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Purdue University developed KVLINC, a framework for 2-bit KV cache quantization in Large Language Models, using a refined Hadamard rotation for values and linear correction adapters for keys. This approach enables up to 3.5x larger batch sizes and 2.55x inference throughput on Llama models, outperforming baselines in long-context tasks while preserving generation quality at an average 2.71 bits per entry.

title: KVLinC : KV Cache Quantization with Hadamard Rotation and Linear Correction

abstract: Long-context inference in large language models is increasingly bottlenecked by the memory and compute cost of the KV-Cache. Low-rank factorization compresses KV projections by writing $W \approx A * B$, where A produces latent KV states and B can be absorbed into downstream weights. In modern RoPE-based LLMs, this absorption fails: RoPE forces latent KV states to be reconstructed to full dimension, reintroducing substantial memory and compute overhead. We propose RoPE-Aligned Pruning (RAP), which prunes entire RoPE-aligned column pairs to preserve RoPE's 2x2 rotation structure, restore B absorption, and eliminate reconstruction. Our evaluation on LLaMA-3-8B and Mistral-7B shows that RAP enables joint reduction of KV-Cache, attention parameters, and FLOPs by 20-30%, all at once, while maintaining strong accuracy. Notably, RAP reduces attention latency to 83% (prefill) and 77% (decode) of baseline.
title: RAP: KV-Cache Compression via RoPE-Aligned Pruning

abstract: The Key-Value (KV) cache is central to the efficiency of transformer-based large language models (LLMs), storing previously computed vectors to accelerate inference. Yet, as sequence length and batch size grow, the cache becomes a major memory bottleneck.
